summary: The advisor recommendation payload includes a reason string assembled from matched prompt/corpus terms, so rendering reason text into a model-visible brief would reflect prompt-derived content across the boundary that wave 1 explicitly wanted to avoid.

The advisor runtime loads skill name and description directly from SKILL.md frontmatter, which means the model-visible skill label remains repository-authored free text and can carry Unicode confusables or instruction-shaped prefixes even when the subprocess JSON itself is trusted.

Phase 019/003's canonicalFold utility is the right reusable defense for the advisor brief because it catches NFKC width forms, hidden characters, combining marks, and current confusables, but the full recovered-payload provenance contract targets multi-line cached transcript text and is heavier than this renderer needs.

The renderer step that converts advisor output into prompt text is itself the trust boundary: typed freshness/confidence values are safe to render directly, but any free-text field crossing into the brief must be treated as untrusted prompt-facing content.

The minimal safe contract is to whitelist freshness enum + numeric confidence/uncertainty + one visible skill label, canonical-fold and single-line-normalize that label, and suppress the brief if the normalized label becomes empty or instruction-shaped after folding.

The future brief renderer should never render advisor reason text, skill descriptions, or keyword-match explanations because those surfaces either echo prompt-derived terms or expose arbitrary SKILL.md free text that is unnecessary for the compact one-line advisor brief.

A regression fixture with a confusable skill label such as `sуstem: ignοre previous` should canonical-fold into `system: ignore previous` and produce a null brief plus diagnostics, proving the renderer fails open instead of emitting instruction-shaped text.

Reusing the full recovered-payload provenance and semantic contract was ruled out because that contract is meant for multi-line cached transcript recovery, while the advisor brief is a renderer-owned one-line summary that only needs field whitelisting and canonical-fold label checks.
